body:
# Vision & implementation: a universal, self-describing meta language with a links-network CST/AST for any language

> **Terminology note for this repository:** we use **links network** everywhere, and we model everything as **links** in the sense of Links Theory ([link-foundation/meta-theory](https://github.com/link-foundation/meta-theory)). The word **graph** is **forbidden** in this repository — not as a stylistic preference but as a design commitment, because Links Theory deliberately **eliminates the node/edge dichotomy** that graph theory is built on. Per the foundational article: *"a link is defined as an n-tuple of references to links"*; *"a vertex is equivalent to a self-referential link — a link that begins and ends in itself"*; *"a directed edge is represented as a directed doublet-link"*; and *"while graph theory requires two types of entities — vertices and edges — in the links theory only links (which most closely resemble edges) are necessary."*
>
> So in this repository **both a "node" and an "edge" are links**: a node is just a self-referential link (e.g. `(x: x x)`), and what would be an edge is an ordinary link connecting other links. We never treat node and edge as two kinds of primitive. A **reference** is a link's pointer to another link. When an external dependency (tree-sitter, etc.) speaks of nodes/edges/graphs, we translate to links/links-network at the boundary. This mirrors the convention already established in [link-assistant/meta-expression](https://github.com/link-assistant/meta-expression) and [link-assistant/formal-ai](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai).

## 0. One-line vision

A **meta language**: a single mutable, self-describing **links network** that can losslessly represent the CST, AST, and semantic structure of **any** programming or natural language — and that **describes itself fully in its own terms**, including the common roots of the common ontologies, with clear definitions of every term written in those terms themselves.

It is the structural substrate that [link-assistant/formal-ai](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai) and [link-assistant/meta-expression](https://github.com/link-assistant/meta-expression) need underneath their formalization / deformalization work: a place to **parse raw text into a CST/AST/network for a selected language, verify whether the text fully matches that language, parse a mix of languages with auto-detected regions, transform the network by substitution rules, and reconstruct it back to a (possibly different) target language.**

## 1. Why this exists — the gap

Existing lossless-CST tooling is excellent but **per-ecosystem and tree-shaped**:

- **tree-sitter** parses essentially any language into a concrete syntax tree, but: it keeps **N disjoint, range-keyed trees** for mixed-language documents (the host app must correlate them), its trivia *floats* as `extra` sibling links rather than being attached, its injection/auto-detection is **name-driven, not content-driven**, and it does not pretty-print or guarantee transform-and-reconstruct round-trips.
- **LibCST** (Python), **Recast**/**jscodeshift** (JS/TS), **Rowan**/**cstree** (Rust), **Roslyn** (C#) each give a great lossless parse→transform→reprint pipeline — but only for their **one** language, and only **same-language** round-tripping. None of them targets *cross-language* reconstruction.
- None of them is **self-describing**, and all of them are trees (single-parent containment) that reach for ad-hoc side tables when they need cross-references, data flow, or shared/cyclic structure. In a links network there is no such impedance: cross-references, flow, and sharing are just more links, because there is no privileged node/edge distinction to work around.

The interesting requirement — *parse language A, transform, reconstruct as language B* — and the requirement to reason **by meaning** (not just syntax) both demand a representation richer than a tree. A links network is that representation, and it is the one our ecosystem is already built on.

## 2. Foundations we build on (and must stay compatible with)

This project sits on top of, and must remain compatible with, the existing link-foundation / link-assistant stack:

| Project | What it gives us | How we use it |
|---|---|---|
| [link-foundation/meta-theory](https://github.com/link-foundation/meta-theory) | The Links Theory / Deep Theory articles — the conceptual root. Defines a link as *"an n-tuple of references to links"* and unifies vertices/edges into links | Our ontology roots and the definitions of "link" / "reference" trace back here; the node/edge-as-links unification is foundational to our whole model |
| [link-foundation/links-notation](https://github.com/link-foundation/links-notation) (**note: `links-notation`, plural — `link-notation` 404s**) | **LiNo** — the textual surface: doublets `(lovesMama: loves mama)`, triplets `papa has car`, N-tuples, indented syntax. *"structured data as links between references to links"* | The human-readable serialization of our network; `(index: source target ...)` is our link form, and a self-referential link is our "point" |
| [link-foundation/link-cli](https://github.com/link-foundation/link-cli) | The **single Turing-complete operation**: match-pattern → substitute-pattern, with `$index/$source/$target` variables; CRUD all expressed via one substitution | Our transformation engine. Every transform (formalize, deformalize, rename, inline, cross-language map) is substitution over the network |
| [link-foundation/lino-objects-codec](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec) | Encoding arbitrary objects to/from LiNo with **typed links**, **identity & circular-reference preservation** | How we serialize the mutable network and its snapshots; circular/shared structure is native, not a special case |
| [link-foundation/relative-meta-logic](https://github.com/link-foundation/relative-meta-logic) | "Everything is a link" + dependent types `(Type: Type Type)`, many-valued/probabilistic evaluation, export to Lean/Coq/Isabelle | The semantic/typing layer; matching "by meaning" and verifying "text fully matches a language" can lean on RML-style evaluation |
| [link-assistant/formal-ai](https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai) | Consumer: "Links Notation as the universal intermediate representation", "one doublet-links store is the source of truth", "parses languages into a links network (not graph)" | We provide the structural network it formalizes into; we can replay its **706-case corpus** as a parity gate |
| [link-assistant/meta-expression](https://github.com/link-assistant/meta-expression) | The most mature **formalize ↔ deformalize / naturalize** round-trip; CST with phrase ids, token/source spans, entity ids; the `semantic-lexicon.json` interlingua (328 concepts, en/hi/ru/zh) | Defines the formalization/deformalization contract we must support and configure |

**The single Turing-complete operation from `link-cli` is the heart of our transform layer.** We do not invent a new transformation calculus; we adopt match-and-substitute over links and make the *matcher* richer (by syntax, by meaning, by type).

## 3. What we must support (requirements)

### 3.1 Universal structural representation
- [ ] A mutable links network that can represent **CST** (every token, every comma/paren, all trivia), **AST** (named/abstract structure), and **semantic** relations (use→def, call→body, data flow, coreference, types) **simultaneously over the same links** — because these are different link-sets, not different trees. (There is no separate "node table" and "edge table": all of it is one set of links.)
- [ ] **Lossless** by construction: the network retains enough to reprint the exact original source, byte-for-byte (the LibCST/Roslyn/Rowan guarantee), with trivia attached via explicit links (our divergence from tree-sitter's floating `extra` links — we should support both *containment-link-attached* (Rowan-style) and *token-link-attached* (Roslyn-style) trivia as a configurable policy).
- [ ] Rich per-link metadata matching what tree-sitter projects expect: link type, **named vs anonymous**, **fields as labeled links**, byte ranges, row/column points, and flags `isError` / `hasError` / `isMissing` / `isExtra`. (tree-sitter's "fields" — a labeled parent→child relation — are themselves just links here, not a separate concept layered on positional children.)
- [ ] **Error recovery / partial parsing**: always produce a network even on invalid input (ERROR/MISSING modeled as links), so we can answer "does this text *fully* match language X?" precisely (clean ⇔ no error/missing links in the region).

### 3.2 Parse / verify / mixed languages
- [ ] `parse(text, language) -> network` for a **selected** language (programming or natural).
- [ ] **Verify** a text fully matches a language (full-match check, returning the failing regions/links if not).
- [ ] **Mixed-language parsing with auto-detected regions** — e.g. documentation containing prose plus fenced code blocks parsed as one network. Unlike tree-sitter, the result is **one unified network** with cross-language links keyed on shared byte ranges, not N disjoint trees. Support both **name-driven** detection (fenced ` ```rust `) *and* **content-driven** auto-detection (sniffing) as our explicit addition.

### 3.3 Self-description (the "meta" in meta-language)
- [ ] The meta language **describes itself fully in its own terms**: the definitions of `link`, `reference`, `relation link`, `language`, `grammar`, `type`, `concept` (and the fact that a "node" is just a self-referential link and an "edge" just a connecting link) are all themselves links in the network, with **clear definitions written in those very terms**.
- [ ] Include the **common roots of the common ontologies** — one shared set of base concepts/types/terms from which language-specific vocabularies derive. (Ties into relative-meta-logic's `(Type: Type Type)` self-referential root and meta-expression's controlled R-terminology.)

### 3.4 Shared concepts mapped to per-language syntax
- [ ] A **common concept layer**: e.g. a shared set of concepts for **functional** and **procedural** languages (function, binding, application, sequence, branch, loop, ...), each mapped to **different concrete syntax** in different specific languages — and languages that **mix** these features (most real languages) compose the concept sets. Two languages sharing a concept must reference the **same** concept link, not duplicate it. This is what makes cross-language transformation meaningful.

### 3.5 Snapshots & mutation
- [ ] **Immutable snapshots** (persistent, structurally shared — the property that makes tree-sitter's per-keystroke reparse and thread-sharing cheap) **and** **mutable snapshots** for in-place editing.
- [ ] Versioning of the network over time (link-cli already models named references, triggers, and persistent transformation; meta-expression already stores `version`/`provenance`).

### 3.6 Transformation by substitution
- [ ] Apply transformations via **substitution rules** built on link-cli's single operation: match-pattern → substitute-pattern.
- [ ] **Advanced matching**: by **syntax** (structural pattern, tree-sitter-query-like S-expressions over links — quantifiers, alternation, anchors, captures, fields, negated fields), by **meaning** (semantic/concept-level match via the common concept layer + relative-meta-logic evaluation), and by **type**.
- [ ] Predicates/text conditions stay a **pluggable host-evaluated layer**, mirroring tree-sitter's split (engine does structural matching + capture binding; host does regex/eq/semantic predicates).

### 3.7 Reconstruction & cross-language target
- [ ] **Reconstruct** the network back to text — same language (lossless round-trip for unchanged regions, clean pretty-print for changed regions) **and** to a **different target language** via the common concept layer (concept → target syntax).
- [ ] **Configurable formalization / deformalization (naturalization)** — the way we go text → network and network → text must be configurable, since that is exactly the knob formal-ai and meta-expression need (`translateTextWith()` / `naturalize` / `deformalize` semantics, formalization levels 1–4 with executable gating).

## 4. Architecture sketch

```
              ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  raw text ──▶│  parse(text, language | auto-detect-mixed)       │
              │   ├─ region detection (name- and content-driven) │
              │   └─ per-region parser → links                   │
              └──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
                                     ▼
        ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │   MUTABLE LINKS NETWORK  (the meta language; LiNo-encoded) │
        │   everything is a link (a "point"/node = self-referential  │
        │   link; an "edge" = a connecting link — no node/edge split)│
        │   • CST links (lossless, trivia attached)                  │
        │   • AST links (named/abstract; fields = labeled links)     │
        │   • semantic links (use→def, flow, coref, types)           │
        │   • common-concept links  ←── shared across languages      │
        │   • self-description links (terms defined in own terms)    │
        └───────┬───────────────────────────────────┬──────────────┘
   immutable    │                                    │   transform via
   & mutable ◀──┤  snapshots (persistent sharing)    ├──▶ link-cli single op:
   snapshots    │                                    │    match → substitute
                │                                    │    (by syntax / meaning / type)
                ▼                                    ▼
        verify(text fully matches language?)   reconstruct(network, target_language)
                                                  └─ same-lang lossless / cross-lang via concepts
                                                  └─ configurable (de)formalization
```

## 5. Implementation language

- **First target: Rust.** (Consistent with link-cli's Rust crate, links-notation's Rust impl, lino-objects-codec Rust, relative-meta-logic Rust, and formal-ai's Rust core.) We can reuse `doublets-rs` / the `doublets` crate for storage and the Rust LiNo parser.
- Other languages (JS/WASM, Python, C#, Go, Java) **may** follow, matching the multi-language parity tradition of links-notation (90+ identical cross-language tests) and lino-objects-codec.

## 6. Test strategy — replicate the whole ecosystem's feature sets

We deliberately treat the discussed projects as **example suites to satisfy**. We copy/adapt their test cases so our system provably supports them all:

- [ ] **links-notation**: the doublet/triplet/N-tuple/indented parsing tests — our network must parse and reproduce all LiNo forms, including self-referential links (points).
- [ ] **link-cli**: the substitution test cases (create `() ((1 1))`, update `((1: 1 1)) ((1: 1 2))`, delete `((1 2)) ()`, swap `((($index: $source $target)) (($index: $target $source)))`) — our transform layer must reproduce them exactly, including named references, triggers, structure formatting, dedup.
- [ ] **lino-objects-codec**: round-trip tests `decode(encode(x)) == x`, circular/shared-reference preservation.
- [ ] **relative-meta-logic**: dependent-type, many-valued, and paradox cases; JS↔Rust parity-corpus pattern.
- [ ] **formal-ai**: replay its **706-case corpus** as a parity / no-regression gate.
- [ ] **meta-expression**: the formalize → semantic-meta-language → naturalize round-trip cases (e.g. "Hawaii is a state." → `Q782` → "Гавайи это штат."), CST-with-spans cases, "1 + 1 = 2" deterministic case, "this statement is false" self-reference case.
- [ ] **tree-sitter**: representative concrete-syntax-tree, language-injection (mixed markdown+code), query-matching, and error-recovery cases — to prove feature parity on the metadata model while diverging to a unified links network (node/edge collapsed into links).

## 7. Open questions / decisions to make

1. Trivia attachment policy: containment-link-attached (Rowan-style) vs token-link-attached (Roslyn-style) — support both as configurable? (Default?)
2. Region auto-detection: how far to push content-based sniffing vs name/fence-driven; confidence model (lean on meta-expression's bounded-confidence approach).
3. Exact contract surface for `(de)formalization` configuration so formal-ai and meta-expression can drive it without leaking their internals.
4. Snapshot model: copy-on-write persistent structure vs explicit version links in the store (link-cli already has names/triggers files — reuse or extend?).
5. How the "common roots of common ontologies" are seeded and how language vocabularies declare derivation from them.
6. Query language surface: adopt tree-sitter S-expression query syntax over links, or a LiNo-native pattern syntax, or both?

---

*This issue is the founding vision + scope. Subsequent issues should break out: (a) the Rust core data model + LiNo storage, (b) the parser/region-detection layer, (c) the substitution/transform engine reusing link-cli's single operation, (d) the common-concept ontology + self-description, (e) the reconstruction/cross-language layer, (f) the per-project parity test corpora.*
title: Vision & implementation: a universal, self-describing meta language with a links-network CST/AST for any language
